mnemonics.title: Asterisk means Go There
mnemonics.content: When you see *ptr, think 'go to the address stored in ptr and get its value' - the asterisk is your ticket to that memory location.

mnemonics.title: Ampersand = Ask Address
mnemonics.content: Use &var to 'ask' a variable for its home address (memory location). Always & when you need to find where something lives.

mnemonics.title: Pointers Point, References Are Aliases
mnemonics.content: Pointers are separate variables that STORE addresses (like a house number on a piece of paper). References are JUST another name for the original variable (like a nickname).

mnemonics.title: NULL = No Place to Go
mnemonics.content: nullptr means 'nowhere' - your pointer isn't pointing to anything valid. Always check for nullptr before dereferencing!

mnemonics.title: Reference Must Be Born Together
mnemonics.content: References MUST be initialized when declared - they can't exist without something to alias. Pointers can be born empty (but shouldn't!).

acronyms.expansion: Pointer - Indirection (*) operator Gets the value
acronyms.usage: Use *ptr to access the value at the address stored in ptr. The indirection/dereference operator 'pig' gets the goods.
Use &var to obtain the memory address of variable var. A.O.A. gives you the location, not the value.

acronyms.expansion: Reference: Alias for Original variable, Cannot be reassigned, Points to same address throughout
acronyms.usage: A reference is like a lifelong nickname - it's always for the same variable and can't be changed to alias something else.

acronyms.expansion: Pointer: Owns an Address, Can be reassigned, Points anywhere
acronyms.usage: Pointers are independent - they can point to different things at different times. They own their address value.

visualTips.concept: Pointer as a House Number on Paper
visualTips.visualization: Draw a box labeled 'ptr' containing a house number (address). Draw another box at that address containing the actual value. Arrow from ptr box points to value box. This shows: pointer HOLDS an address, not the value itself.
Draw one box for 'age' with value 25. Draw a second tag attached to the SAME box labeled 'years'. Both names refer to the SAME single box - there's only one piece of data, just two names for it.
Show: &x gives address → store in ptr → *ptr gives back x. Draw a cycle: [x] →(&)→ [address] →(*)→ [x]. These inverse operations cancel each other out.
Draw a pointer box with 'NULL' or 'nullptr' written in it, with a dotted line leading nowhere. Add a warning sign: 'Don't follow this path!' Always check before dereferencing.

analogies.analogy: Think of a pointer as a house address written on a piece of paper - the paper (pointer) is separate from the house (data), and you can throw away that paper and write a different address. A reference is like having a nickname - you're always referring to the SAME person, and you can't change who the nickname belongs to.
It's like using a GPS coordinates to find a treasure. The pointer holds the coordinates (address), and the * operator is the command to 'Go there and dig!' to retrieve what's buried at that location.
It's like asking someone for their mailing address. You have the person (variable), and you use & to get their address so you can send mail (store the address elsewhere, like in a pointer).
A pointer without initialization is like a GPS with no destination entered - it's dangerous to follow because you don't know where it'll take you. Always set a valid address or nullptr first.
If 'tv' is your TV and you create a reference 'remote = tv', pressing buttons on 'remote' does exactly what pressing buttons on 'tv' would do - they're the SAME device, just with different names.
